EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT NOTE
Date/Time: 2026-02-26 13:45  |  Facility: Cityview Medical Center (synthetic)
Attending: Priya Natarajan, MD  |  Resident/APP: Jordan Li, PA-C
PATIENT: Jordan, Avery (Fictional)   MRN: 000123456   DOB: 1986-07-14   Age: 39   Sex: F

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
- Shortness of breath, cough, and fever

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
39 y/o female with history of asthma, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and depression
presenting with 3 days of
productive cough, subjective fevers, pleuritic chest discomfort, and worsening dyspnea on
exertion. She reports
increased albuterol use (6-8 times/day) with partial relief. Denies hemoptysis. No recent
travel. No known sick
contacts. Reports poor oral intake since yesterday and lightheadedness when standing.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: +fever, +fatigue
- Respiratory: +cough, +wheeze, +dyspnea
- Cardiovascular: -palpitations, -leg swelling
- GI: -vomiting, -diarrhea
- GU: -dysuria
- Neuro: -focal weakness
All other systems reviewed and negative except as above.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Asthma (moderate persistent)
- Hypertension
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus
- Hyperlipidemia
- Depression/anxiety

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Appendectomy (2004)

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin: "rash as a child" (history unclear)
- No known food allergies
HOME MEDICATIONS (per patient, may be incomplete):
- Albuterol HFA 90 mcg: 2 puffs q4h PRN wheeze
- Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50: 1 inhalation BID (admits missed doses)
- Metformin ER 1000 mg BID
- Lisinopril 10 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
- Sertraline 50 mg daily

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Tobacco: never
- Alcohol: rare
- Drugs: denies
- Housing: stable; lives with partner
VITAL SIGNS (triage):
- Temp 38.3 C
- HR 112
- BP 146/88
- RR 24
- SpO2 92% on room air (improved to 95% on 2L NC)

PHYSICAL EXAM:
General: uncomfortable, speaking in short sentences
HEENT: mucous membranes dry
Lungs: diffuse expiratory wheeze; coarse crackles right base
CV: tachycardic, regular; no murmurs
Abd: soft, non-tender
Ext: no edema; no calf tenderness
Neuro: AOx3; no focal deficits

LABS:
- CBC: WBC 14.8 K/uL (neutrophil predominant), Hgb 12.7 g/dL, Plt 312 K/uL
- CMP: Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Cr 0.78 mg/dL, Glu 238 mg/dL
- Lactate: 1.6 mmol/L
- Troponin I: <0.01 ng/mL
- BNP: 28 pg/mL
- VBG: pH 7.36 / pCO2 46 / HCO3 25
- Respiratory viral PCR: negative (influenza A/B, RSV, SARS-CoV-2)

IMAGING:
- CXR: Right lower lobe patchy opacity concerning for pneumonia; no pleural effusion.
- EKG: Sinus tachycardia; no ischemic changes.

ED COURSE / MEDICAL DECISION MAKING:
Concern for community-acquired pneumonia with asthma exacerbation. Started nebulized
bronchodilators x3,
IV fluids, prednisone, and empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone + azithromycin). Given
borderline hypoxia and tachypnea,
admit to medicine for observation and ongoing treatment.

IMPRESSION:
1) Community-acquired pneumonia (RLL)
2) Asthma exacerbation
3) Hyperglycemia (T2DM) in setting of infection and steroids
4) Dehydration

DISPOSITION:
- Admit to Medicine/Telemetry
- Oxygen to maintain SpO2 > 92%
- Continue bronchodilators; start controller optimization inpatient

CARDIOLOGY DIAGNOSTIC REPORT
Test: 12-Lead ECG
Date/Time: 2026-02-26 13:45
Rate: 112 bpm
Rhythm: Sinus tachycardia
Axis: Normal
Intervals: PR 152 ms; QRS 84 ms; QTc 438 ms
ST-T: No acute ST elevation or depression
Impression:
- Sinus tachycardia, otherwise normal ECG.

INPATIENT HISTORY & PHYSICAL (H&P)
Admit Date/Time: 2026-02-26 17:20
Service: Internal Medicine  |  Location: 5N Telemetry (synthetic)
Admitting Provider: Samuel Ortega, MD

IDENTIFYING INFORMATION:
Patient: Jordan, Avery (Fictional)  MRN: 000123456  DOB: 1986-07-14  Age: 39  Sex: F

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
- "I can't catch my breath and I keep coughing."

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient presents from ED with suspected community-acquired pneumonia and asthma
exacerbation.
Symptoms began 3 days prior to admission. She endorses wheezing, productive cough (yellow
sputum),
and fever/chills. Reports chest tightness worse with coughing. No history of prior
intubation.
She has been inconsistent with ICS/LABA due to cost and perceived side effects (hoarseness).

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Asthma (moderate persistent); last hospitalization 2019
- Hypertension
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus
- Hyperlipidemia
- Major depressive disorder / generalized anxiety

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Appendectomy (2004)
ALLERGIES (reconciled):
- Penicillin: rash as a child (no anaphylaxis). Patient unsure if true allergy.

MEDICATIONS PRIOR TO ADMISSION:
- Metformin ER 1000 mg BID
- Lisinopril 10 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
- Sertraline 50 mg daily
- Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 BID (inconsistent adherence)
- Albuterol inhaler PRN

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Father: CAD, type 2 diabetes
- Mother: asthma

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Never smoker; no vaping
- Rare alcohol; denies illicit drugs
- Works in customer support; no occupational exposures noted

PHYSICAL EXAM ON ADMISSION:
Vitals: Temp 38.1 C, HR 104, BP 138/84, RR 22, SpO2 95% on 2L NC
General: mildly ill-appearing
Lungs: diffuse wheeze; crackles at right base; prolonged expiratory phase
CV: tachycardic, regular rhythm
Abd: soft, non-tender
Neuro: alert; oriented x3

DATA REVIEW:
- CXR: RLL opacity consistent with pneumonia
- WBC 14.8, glucose 238, Na 134, creatinine 0.78
- Viral PCR negative

ASSESSMENT & PLAN:
1) Community-acquired pneumonia (RLL)
   - Continue ceftriaxone 1 g IV q24h + azithromycin 500 mg IV daily
   - Blood cultures x2; sputum culture if able
   - Incentive spirometry; ambulation as tolerated
2) Asthma exacerbation
   - Prednisone 40 mg daily x5 days (first dose in ED)
   - Scheduled duonebs q4h while awake; albuterol PRN
   - Restart controller therapy; respiratory therapy education
   - Peak flow monitoring
3) Hypoxemia
   - Wean oxygen as tolerated; goal SpO2 > 92%
   - Consider CT angiography if worsening or suspicion for PE (currently low suspicion)
4) Type 2 diabetes with hyperglycemia
   - Hold metformin while inpatient (acute illness)
   - Start basal/bolus insulin protocol; check glucose AC/HS
   - Order HbA1c
5) Hypertension
   - Continue lisinopril 10 mg daily; monitor BP
6) Depression/anxiety
   - Continue sertraline 50 mg daily
7) VTE prophylaxis
   - Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily
8) Disposition / goals
   - Expected LOS 2-3 days, discharge home when off oxygen, improving symptoms

RADIOLOGY REPORT
Study: Chest X-Ray (2 Views)
Date/Time: 2026-02-26 14:05
Indication: cough, fever, dyspnea
Findings:
- Patchy right lower lobe airspace opacity.
- No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
- Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits.
Impression:
1) Right lower lobe opacity concerning for pneumonia.
2) No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.
Radiologist: Elaine Morrison, MD

RADIOLOGY REPORT
Study: CT Angiography Chest (PE Protocol)
Date/Time: 2026-02-26 20:30
Indication: persistent tachycardia and hypoxemia; rule out pulmonary embolism
Technique:
IV contrast administered. Axial images acquired with multiplanar reformats.
Findings:
- No evidence of pulmonary embolus to the segmental level.
- Right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms.
- Mild bronchial wall thickening consistent with reactive airway disease.
- No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
Impression:
1) Negative for pulmonary embolism.
2) Right lower lobe consolidation consistent with pneumonia.
Radiologist: Elaine Morrison, MD

CARDIOLOGY CONSULT NOTE
Date/Time: 2026-02-26 21:15
Consultant: Victor Shah, MD (synthetic)
Reason for consult:
- Persistent tachycardia during pneumonia/asthma exacerbation
Summary:
Patient with HR 95-115 during first 24h of admission. No chest pain at rest. Troponin
negative.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia. BNP normal. No signs of volume overload.
Assessment:
- Sinus tachycardia likely secondary to infection, beta-agonist treatments, and dehydration.
- Low suspicion for acute coronary syndrome.
Recommendations:
- Continue treatment of underlying infection/asthma.
- Ensure adequate hydration.
- Minimize unnecessary beta-agonist frequency once stable.
- If tachycardia persists after clinical improvement, consider TSH and outpatient
  monitoring.

Date/Time: 2026-02-27 15:10
Dietitian: A. Singh, RD (synthetic)
Reason for consult:
- Diabetes with hyperglycemia; diet education requested
Assessment:
- Appetite improving; reports irregular meals and frequent takeout due to work schedule.
- Estimated needs: 1800-2000 kcal/day (maintenance), protein 70-90 g/day.
- Barriers: time, cost, limited cooking.
Plan:
- Recommend consistent carbohydrate diet while inpatient.
- Encourage high-fiber carbs (whole grains, legumes), lean proteins, vegetables.
- Provide quick meal ideas and grocery list.
- Follow-up PRN.

parent_title: 5) Hospital Course: Progress Notes, Nursing, RT, Pharmacy
text:
DIABETES EDUCATION NOTE
Date/Time: 2026-02-27 14:40
Educator: N. Garcia, CDCES (synthetic)
Topics covered:
- Sick day rules: monitor glucose more frequently during illness; maintain hydration; call
  clinician if persistent vomiting.
- Steroid-associated hyperglycemia: temporary insulin needs may increase while on
  prednisone.
- Meal planning: consistent carbohydrates; plate method; avoid sugar-sweetened beverages.
- Home monitoring: target fasting 80-130 mg/dL; 2-hour postprandial <180 mg/dL
  (individualized).
Patient goals (self-identified):
1) Check fasting glucose daily for the next 2 weeks.
2) Replace one sugar-sweetened drink per day with water.
3) Schedule follow-up with primary care for medication review.
Handouts provided:
- Glucose log sheet
- Plate method infographic (synthetic)

- Discharge home today if stable after noon ambulation check.

DISCHARGE SUMMARY
Discharge Date/Time: 2026-02-28 15:10
Attending: Samuel Ortega, MD
Primary Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe)
Secondary Diagnoses: Asthma exacerbation; type 2 diabetes with hyperglycemia; hypertension;
depression/anxiety

HOSPITAL COURSE:
Patient admitted with fever, cough, hypoxemia, and wheezing. Imaging demonstrated RLL
consolidation. Viral PCR was negative.
Treated with ceftriaxone + azithromycin and systemic steroids for asthma exacerbation.
Oxygen requirement improved; weaned to room air
by discharge. Blood cultures remained negative. Glucose elevations were managed with insulin
while on prednisone.
Pharmacy assisted with medication reconciliation and cost-conscious inhaler planning.

DISCHARGE CONDITION:
Stable, afebrile, breathing comfortably on room air. Ambulating without desaturation.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
- Azithromycin 250 mg PO daily x2 days (complete course)
- Cefpodoxime 200 mg PO BID x3 days (complete course)  [Note: chosen due to non-severe
  penicillin rash history]
- Prednisone 40 mg PO daily to complete 5-day burst (2 days remaining)
- Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50: 1 inhalation BID (controller)
- Albuterol HFA: 2 puffs q4h PRN wheeze (rescue)
- Metformin ER 1000 mg PO BID (restart)
- Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
- Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
- Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
- Benzonatate 100 mg PO TID PRN cough x7 days

FOLLOW-UP:
- Primary Care: within 7 days
- Pulmonology: 2-4 weeks (asthma optimization)
- Diabetes education referral placed

RETURN PRECAUTIONS:
Call or return to ED for worsening shortness of breath, persistent fever > 38.0 C, chest
pain, confusion, or inability to keep fluids down.

PENDING RESULTS:
- Final sputum culture (if obtained): pending at discharge (expected no growth)
AFTER VISIT SUMMARY (AVS) - Discharge Instructions (Synthetic)
Discharge Date: 2026-02-28
Why you were in the hospital:
- Pneumonia and an asthma flare causing shortness of breath.
What we did:
- Antibiotics to treat pneumonia
- Steroids and breathing treatments for asthma
- Oxygen (now no longer needed)
- Insulin temporarily for high blood sugars while on steroids
Your new or changed medicines:
- Continue controller inhaler every day.
- Prednisone: finish remaining doses as prescribed.
- Antibiotics: finish the full course.
Follow-up appointments:
- Primary care: within 7 days
- Pulmonology: within 2-4 weeks (if scheduled)
- Diabetes educator: referral placed
Call 911 or go to the ER if:
- Severe trouble breathing
- New chest pain
- Confusion or fainting
- Blue lips or face
- High fever that does not improve
Notes:
This AVS is synthetic and for NLP/RAG testing only.
PATIENT EDUCATION HANDOUT: PNEUMONIA (Adult) - Discharge Instructions (Synthetic)
What is pneumonia?
- Pneumonia is an infection in the lungs that can cause cough, fever, and trouble breathing.
How to recover at home:
- Take all antibiotics exactly as prescribed until finished.
- Drink fluids to avoid dehydration.
- Rest, but also take short walks as tolerated to prevent deconditioning.
- Use your inhalers as instructed (controller daily; rescue only as needed).
When to call your clinic urgently:
- Fever >= 38.0 C after you have been improving
- Worsening shortness of breath or wheezing
- Chest pain that is new or getting worse
- Coughing up blood
- Confusion or severe weakness
- Inability to keep liquids down
Asthma-specific reminders:
- Controller inhaler: use every day even if you feel well.
- Rescue inhaler: use for sudden symptoms; if you need it more often than usual, call your
  clinician.
- Avoid triggers (smoke, strong fragrances, cold air, dust).
Follow-up:
- See primary care within 1 week.
- Consider pulmonary follow-up for asthma control.
This handout is for education only and is not a substitute for medical advice.

REASON FOR VISIT:
- Post-hospital follow-up for pneumonia/asthma exacerbation
- Diabetes management

INTERVAL HISTORY:
Patient reports breathing is "much better" and cough has nearly resolved. Completed
antibiotics and prednisone.
Using controller inhaler daily with spacer. Albuterol use down to 1-2 times/week.
No recurrent fever.
Diabetes: Home fasting glucose readings range 140-180 mg/dL. Reports diet has been
inconsistent.
Denies hypoglycemia.

MEDICATIONS:
- Metformin ER 1000 mg BID (taking)
- Lisinopril 10 mg daily
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
- Sertraline 50 mg daily
- Fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 BID
- Albuterol PRN

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin: non-severe rash as a child (documented; not anaphylaxis)

VITALS:
- BP 128/76
- HR 82
- SpO2 97% RA
- Weight 86.4 kg

ASSESSMENT/PLAN:
1) Pneumonia, resolved
   - No further antibiotics. Repeat CXR in 6 weeks only if symptoms recur.
2) Asthma
   - Continue ICS/LABA twice daily + spacer.
   - Provide written asthma action plan. Trigger avoidance reviewed.
3) Type 2 diabetes, uncontrolled
   - HbA1c from hospitalization: 8.1%
   - Continue metformin.
   - Start GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) 0.25 mg weekly x4 weeks, then 0.5 mg weekly if tolerated.
   - Counsel on GI side effects and slow titration.
4) Hypertension
   - Continue lisinopril 10 mg daily.
5) Preventive care
   - Vaccines discussed (influenza annually; pneumococcal per guidelines).
PATIENT PORTAL MESSAGES (Synthetic)
Thread Date: 2026-03-18
Patient message:
"Hi, my controller inhaler is too expensive at my pharmacy. Is there a cheaper option? Also
my cough is still lingering."
Clinic response (RN):
"Thanks for reaching out. We notified your provider. In the meantime, please continue rescue
inhaler as prescribed and call if breathing worsens."
Provider response (NP):
"We can switch to a lower-cost inhaled steroid option. I sent a new prescription and a
spacer. Please use it daily. If cough persists beyond 4-6 weeks or you develop fever,
schedule a visit."

No fever. Mild wheeze at night. Using controller inhaler inconsistently again due to cost.
Denies chest pain, hemoptysis, or leg swelling.

VITALS:

- HR 86
- BP 124/78
- RR 16
- SpO2 98% RA

EXAM:

- Otherwise normal

2) Asthma - adherence barrier

- No antibiotics indicated today; warning signs reviewed.
- Consider repeat imaging if cough persists >6 weeks or if new fever/shortness of breath.

Provider: Megan Wallace, MD (synthetic)

REFERRAL REASON:
- Asthma optimization after recent hospitalization

HISTORY:
- Asthma since childhood. Triggers: cold air, viral URIs, dust.
- Recent hospitalization for pneumonia + asthma exacerbation.
- Currently: daytime symptoms 1-2x/week, nighttime symptoms 1x/week, rescue inhaler 2x/week.
- No ED visits since discharge.
PFTs (today):

- FVC: 2.85 L (86% predicted)
- FEV1/FVC: 0.72
- Significant bronchodilator response: +14%

ASSESSMENT:
- Moderate persistent asthma with adherence challenges.

PLAN:

  covered.
- Spacer and inhaler technique re-reviewed.
- Consider allergy testing if symptoms increase.
- Vaccination counseling: influenza annually; pneumococcal per risk.
- Follow-up 3 months.
